1: Another precise date: the beginning of the reign (bereshit mamlekhet) of Jehoiakim — this places the temple sermon early in Jehoiakim's reign (608-607 BCE), providing the historical context that ch 7 (the sermon itself) lacks. The narrator preserves the context of what must have been a life-threatening crisis for Jeremiah.
2: עֲמֹד בַּחֲצַר בֵּית-יְהוָה (amod bachatzar beit-YHWH, stand in the court of YHWH's house): Jeremiah is instructed to speak in the most public sacred space — the outer court where all who come to worship can hear. The message is not for a private audience but for maximum public impact. אַל-תִּגְרַע דָּבָר (al-tigra' davar, do not hold back a word): prophetic integrity requires complete disclosure, even when the message is dangerous.
3: Perhaps (ulay) they will listen and turn (yashuvhu) — the conditional clause preserves genuine human freedom while announcing inevitable judgment. YHWH does not pronounce the outcome as predetermined; the possibility of repentance is real. נִחַמְתִּי (nichamti, I will relent): YHWH's repentance/change of mind is real — cf. Jonah 3:9-10 (God relented from the disaster he had threatened Nineveh).
4: If you do not listen to walk in my law (torati) — the covenant condition restated with Deuteronomic vocabulary. The prophets are sent as the covenant's enforcement mechanism; ignoring them = ignoring the covenant terms.
5: My servants the prophets (avadai haneviim) — the prophets are again YHWH's servants, sent persistently and urgently (rising early, v4). But you have not listened — the same indictment as ch 25:4.
6: שִׁלֹה (Shiloh) as the precedent: the first sanctuary where the ark was kept (Josh 18:1; 1 Sam 1-4) was destroyed. Shiloh's destruction (ca. 1050 BCE, though the timing is debated) is Jeremiah's proof that YHWH will destroy his own sanctuary if covenant-keeping fails. The threat is not empty rhetoric — there is historical precedent. Cf. Ps 78:60.
7: The priests, prophets, and all the people heard Jeremiah — the full spectrum of religious leadership. Their hearing sets up the trial: they are witnesses to what was said, and their subsequent judgment will reveal whether they comprehend YHWH's word or defend their own institutional interests.
8: As soon as Jeremiah finished speaking, the priests and prophets seized him (yakhaziqu bo) saying: 'You must die!' (mot tamut): the infinitive absolute of death — the same formula used in death-penalty declarations. The institutional religious leadership responds with violence when their security is threatened.
9: Why have you prophesied in YHWH's name saying this house will become like Shiloh? — the charge is not false prophecy (they don't address truth) but institutional blasphemy against the temple. The people massed against Jeremiah (vayiqahel kol-ha'am) — a crowd becomes a mob.
10: The officials of Judah come up from the king's house to the temple — the civil leadership enters as a higher court. The trial moves from religious to civil authority, reflecting the institutional complexity of the crisis.
11: The priests and prophets speak to the officials and all the people: 'This man deserves the death penalty' (mishpat mavet la'ish hazeh) — a formal legal verdict. The accusation: he has prophesied against this city as you have heard with your own ears. The charge conflates the prophet's message with an attack on the institution.
12: Jeremiah's defense: יְהוָה שְׁלָחַנִי (YHWH shelachani, YHWH sent me) — the authority claim. The divine commission is the basis of prophetic immunity; killing the prophet is killing YHWH's messenger. The defense is Christological in its structure: the sent one's authority derives entirely from the sender.
13: Now amend your ways (hatzivu darkeykhem) and deeds (ma'alelekhem) and obey the voice of YHWH your God — the prophetic appeal is still open even at the moment of threatened execution. Jeremiah preaches while on trial for his life; YHWH may relent.
14: הִנְנִי בְיֶדְכֶם (hineni beydekhem, I am in your hands): Jeremiah submits to their jurisdiction — he does not claim prophetic immunity from civil process. Do to me what seems good and right to you. The passive acceptance of possible death mirrors a pattern: the servant who delivers YHWH's word trusts YHWH with the outcome.
15: דַּם נָקִי (dam naqi, innocent blood): the legal category — executing Jeremiah would bring innocent blood on the city and its inhabitants, on YHWH himself (al nafshoteykhem ve'al ha'ir hazot). In covenant law, shedding innocent blood defiles the land (Num 35:33-34). The prophets who plotted against Jeremiah in Anathoth (ch 11) were seeking to shed innocent blood.
16: The officials and all the people to the priests and prophets: 'This man does not deserve the death penalty — he has spoken to us in the name of YHWH our God.' The civil officials recognize the distinction between institutional threat and genuine prophetic authority; they acquit.
17: Some of the elders of the land (ziqnei ha'aretz) rose to speak — the elders invoked as historical witnesses. They provide the precedent that Jeremiah's message is not unprecedented or deserving of death; it follows the pattern of true prophecy.
18: מִיכָה הַמֹּרַשְׁתִּי (Mikhah hamMorashthi, Micah of Moresheth): Micah the prophet (Mic 3:12) is cited as a legal precedent — his contemporary was Hezekiah. צִיּוֹן שָׂדֶה תֵחָרֵשׁ (Tzyon sadeh techaresh, Zion will be plowed as a field): Micah's exact words (Mic 3:12) are quoted — the memory of prophetic words was preserved and invoked as legal precedent generations later.
19: Did Hezekiah put Micah to death? — the rhetorical historical question. Hezekiah feared YHWH (yare' et-YHWH) and sought (vayechal) his favor — the king's appropriate response to prophetic warning. And YHWH relented (vayyinnachem) of the disaster announced. The precedent proves: repentance is possible, killing prophets is wrong, and YHWH responds to genuine fear of him.
20: The case of Uriah son of Shemaiah — a contemporary prophet who also prophesied against Jerusalem and Judah. He fled to Egypt when Jehoiakim sought to kill him — the contrast with Jeremiah who stayed.
21: Jehoiakim sent men to Egypt — Elnathan son of Achbor and others — who brought Uriah back from Egypt. The international reach of royal power to silence prophets demonstrates the danger Jeremiah faced.
22: They brought Uriah from Egypt and presented him to King Jehoiakim, who killed him with the sword — the executive execution of a prophet. The contrast with Jeremiah is stark: same message, similar threat, different outcomes. Providence rather than merit determines survival.
23: His body was thrown into the burial place of the common people (qivrei benei ha'am) — a dishonorable burial that adds shame to death. The contrast: Jeremiah is preserved to fulfill his calling; Uriah's death is a warning of what prophets risked.
24: אֲחִיקָם בֶּן-שָׁפָן (Achiqam ben-Shaphan, Ahikam son of Shaphan): the Shaphan family were consistent supporters of both Josiah's reform and Jeremiah. Ahikam's protection (lo natan oto beyad ha'am lehammito, he did not give him into the hand of the people to put him to death) was decisive. Providence working through covenant-faithful individuals preserves the prophet for his mission.
